flashcards.front: What ACID properties are directly enforced by recovery techniques?
flashcards.back: Atomicity (transactions complete fully or not at all) and Durability (committed changes persist)

flashcards.front: What is the purpose of write-ahead logging (WAL)?
flashcards.back: Ensures log records are written to stable storage before corresponding database updates

flashcards.front: What does ARIES stand for in recovery?
flashcards.back: Algorithm for Recovery and Isolation Exploiting Semantics

flashcards.front: What is a dirty page in DBMS?
flashcards.back: A page in buffer pool that has been modified but not yet written to disk

flashcards.front: What is the purpose of checkpoints?
flashcards.back: To limit recovery time by creating synchronization points between logs and database
Recovery technique that maintains two page tables (current and shadow) for atomic updates

flashcards.front: What are the three phases of ARIES recovery?
What is the difference between undo and redo operations?
flashcards.back: Undo reverses uncommitted changes, Redo reapplies committed changes not yet written to disk
Chronological record of all database operations with before/after values
Failure of secondary storage devices requiring restore from backups
Steal: Allows writing uncommitted data to disk. No-steal: Prohibits it
Checkpointing technique that doesn't require quiescent system state
